steps.title: Hoe laat is het? 🕐
steps.desc: 'What time is it?' — essential for daily life! Dutch telling of time is a bit different from English.

steps.example: Hoe laat is het? Drie uur.
steps.exampleEn: What time is it? Three o'clock.
steps.note: A het-word. 'Uur' for whole hours. 'Om drie uur' = at three o'clock. 'Om hoe laat?' = at what time?
A de-word. Plural: 'minuten'. Related to English 'minute' — same Latin root.
Same concept as English! 'Over' = past. 'Kwart over tien' = quarter past ten.
'Voor' = to/before. 'Kwart voor acht' = quarter to eight. Same logic as English.
⚠️ BIGGEST TRAP! 'Half drie' = 2:30, NOT 3:30! Dutch counts toward the NEXT hour. Half drie = halfway TO three.

steps.title: ⚠️ The Dutch half-hour trap
steps.text:
This catches EVERY foreigner:

🕑 Half drie = 2:30 (halfway to 3)
🕒 Half vier = 3:30 (halfway to 4)
🕓 Half vijf = 4:30 (halfway to 5)

Think of it as: halfway TO the hour.

More examples:
🕐 Half twee = 1:30
🕛 Half een = 12:30
🕕 Half zeven = 6:30

Dutch people also say:
• Tien over half drie = 2:40 (10 past half-three)
• Vijf voor half drie = 2:25 (5 before half-three)

This is genuinely confusing — even Germans (who have a similar system) sometimes get lost!

deepDive.title: The full Dutch clock system
deepDive.text:
The complete system revolves around quarters and halves:

2:00 — twee uur
2:05 — vijf over twee
2:10 — tien over twee
2:15 — kwart over twee
2:20 — tien voor half drie ⚠️
2:25 — vijf voor half drie
2:30 — half drie ⚠️
2:35 — vijf over half drie
2:40 — tien over half drie
2:45 — kwart voor drie
2:50 — tien voor drie
2:55 — vijf voor drie
3:00 — drie uur

After 2:15, everything references either 'half drie' or 'drie uur'.

In daily life, many Dutch people just use digital time: 'Het is 14:35' — especially for appointments.

steps.q: How do you say 'quarter past seven'?

steps.title: My Daily Routine 🌅
steps.desc: What do you do every day? Wake up, eat, work, sleep — learn the verbs of daily life.

steps.title: Separable Verbs
steps.text:
Some Dutch verbs have a prefix that separates in main clauses:

opstaan → Ik sta om 7 uur op.
weggaan → Ik ga om 8 uur weg.
aankomen → Hij komt morgen aan.

The prefix goes to the END of the main clause.
In perfect tense, 'ge-' goes BETWEEN: op-ge-staan

Full explanation → Grammar tab → A2 → Separable Verbs

steps.example: Ik sta om zeven uur op.
steps.exampleEn: I get up at seven o'clock.
steps.note: A separable verb! 'Op' goes to the end: 'Ik sta op' not 'Ik opsta'. This is a key pattern.
From 'ontbijt' (breakfast). Dutch breakfasts are simple: bread with toppings, coffee/tea. Quick and practical!

steps.example: Ik werk van negen tot vijf.
steps.exampleEn: I work from nine to five.
steps.note: 'Van...tot...' = from...to... Dutch people are known for their work-life balance — many work 4-day weeks!

steps.example: Ik slaap acht uur per nacht.
steps.exampleEn: I sleep eight hours per night.
steps.note: Irregular past: 'sliep' (slept). Related to English 'sleep'. 'Slaap lekker!' = Sleep well!

steps.title: 🔄 Separable verbs in action
steps.text:
'Opstaan' (to get up) shows how separable verbs work:

✅ Ik sta om 7 uur op. (main clause — prefix to end)
✅ ...omdat ik om 7 uur opsta. (subordinate — stays together)
✅ Ik ben om 7 uur opgestaan. (perfect — ge- goes in between)

More daily separable verbs:
• aankleden — to get dressed (Ik kleed me aan.)
• weggaan — to leave (Ik ga om 8 uur weg.)
• thuiskomen — to come home (Ik kom om 6 uur thuis.)
• opruimen — to tidy up (Ik ruim de kamer op.)

steps.q: 'Ik sta om zeven uur op' — what is the infinitive?
steps.hint: The prefix 'op' separated and went to the end
To get up = opstaan → ik sta...op

steps.title: Regular Verb Drill
steps.text:
You've seen the conjugation pattern before. Quick refresher:

werken → stem: werk

ik werk
jij werkt
hij/zij werkt
wij werken

Remember: the stem is the infinitive minus -en.
Then add -t for jij/hij/zij, or use the full infinitive for wij/jullie/zij.

Now try it yourself! 👇

steps.title: Days & Planning 📅
steps.desc: Master the days of the week and make simple plans. 'What are you doing on Saturday?'

steps.note: Moon-day! 'Maan' = moon, 'dag' = day. Same origin as English Monday (moon-day).
Named after the Norse god Tyr (Thing). All Dutch days end in '-dag' (day).
Named after the god Wodan (=Odin). Same as English Wednesday (Woden's day).
Thunder-day! 'Donder' = thunder. Named after Thor/Donar. English: Thursday (Thor's day).
Named after the goddess Freya/Frigga. English: Friday (Freya's day). TGIF → 'God zij dank, het is vrijdag!'
From Latin 'Saturni dies' (Saturn's day). Same as English Saturday. 'Boodschappen doen' = grocery shopping.
Sun-day! 'Zon' = sun. A day of rest. Many shops are closed or have limited hours on Sunday.

steps.title: 📅 Making plans
steps.text:
How to talk about days and plans:

📅 Op maandag werk ik. — On Monday I work.
📅 Wat doe je op zaterdag? — What do you do on Saturday?
📅 Ik ga naar de markt. — I go to the market.
📅 Zullen we vrijdag afspreken? — Shall we meet on Friday?

Notice: days of the week are NOT capitalized in Dutch!
• maandag (not Maandag)
• dinsdag (not Dinsdag)

This is different from English!

steps.q: Which day is named after Thor/Donar?

steps.hint: The typical Dutch grocery shopping day
